As Scotland’s largest, loudest, and most populated city, Glasgow is one of Britain’s premier vacation destinations. There are countless things to do and places to explore in around the city. It overlooks the River Clyde on the west coast of Scotland and is known for being on the cutting edge of emerging trends.
 
While Visiting Glasgow, you could easily fill your time exploring the city’s diverse neighborhoods. If you’re looking to engage in a little ‘retail therapy’ to relax your nerves, there are numerous fantastic shops, galleries, boutiques, and marketplaces to shop at. Glasgow is also known for its wealth of dining and entertainment options.

From music venues to museums and theatres, Glasgow is a place where art, history, and culture are alive and well. Pretty much any day of the week, at all hours from dusk to dawn, there is always something to do. Watch this video to see our picks of the best things to do in Glasgow, Scotland.

the city from music venues to museums and theaters Glasgow is a place where art history and culture are alive and well keep watching as eukation presents the best things to do in Glasgow Scotland Glasgow Botanic Gardens as one of the prettiest green spaces in Glasgow this attraction is bursting at the seams with history botany and eye grabbing architecture established in 1841 as a part of the University of Glasgow the gardens were put under the control of the city in 1891. in 1873 one of the garden's most distinctive buildings The domed Glass House kibble Palace was built a few years later the equally striking Main Range teak glass house was erected both these beautiful buildings have been exquisitely preserved and are brimming with exotic plants from all over the world Buchanan Street one of the best places to begin your glasgow's sightseeing tour is the famous and historic thoroughfare Buchanan Street traces its history back to the 18th century and is home to a wide array of Victorian era architecture as well as some of the city's most famous upmarket shops and eateries it's really the place to begin your exploration of Glasgow the street stretches from the Buchanan galleries on the Northern end of town and extends down to Saint Enoch Square to the South you could easily walk the full length in about 15 minutes or so if you're in a rush but we highly recommend taking your time as there are many unexpected things that await you in the surrounding spaces side streets and arcades in addition to the Buchanan galleries you'll also come upon landmarks like Glasgow Central Station St George's Tron Church Nelson Mandela place and Royal exchange Square the latter is home to a few of glasgow's most impressive public buildings as well as the gallery of Modern Art and the statue of the Duke of Wellington City Center mural Trail Glasgow is a city that Embraces its abundance of street art if you follow this Trail you can admire new numerous pieces of urban artwork that liven up countless walls of the city's Center a few of the most notable Creations have been crafted by acclaimed Street artists like art pistol ayek and Ali Wiley one of the most famous is Spaceman on new wind that piece was created by Wiley and ricoat another standout is Rogue one's shadow puppets which can be found on the calcutten's underpass huntarian Museum and art gallery if you're interested in exploring the wonders of the Arts sciences and history look no further than Scotland's oldest public museum the huntarian museum has a vast collection that includes many Hands-On and must-see exhibits including a permanent Gallery dedicated to the famous antonine wall the museum also houses the world's most extensive collection of works by James McNeil Whistler if you're a fan of the classics the museum is home to many iconic art pieces such as Rembrandt's entombment history Buffs will Marvel at the exhibits dedicated to Mankind's most significant scientific discoveries and advancements while the exhibits that relate to Zoological paleontology logical and geological specimens are equally eye-opening Kellen Grove art gallery and Museum this fantastic attraction happens to be one of the most visited art galleries and museums on the planet it's home to a vast array of more than 8 000 exhibits covering everything from Salvador Dali's Christ of Saint John of the Cross to a real Spitfire aircraft that saw action in World War II throughout the massive Museum you'll see items like medieval suits of armor enormous dinosaur skeletons and stunning works of art by the Old Dutch Masters Scottish colorists and French Impressionists Glasgow Cathedral ever since it was consecrated in 1197 this church has remained a sturdy and enduring example of Scottish Gothic architecture if you think its exterior is impressive just wait until you take a peek inside the cathedral is notable for having one of the most expansive collections of post-war stained glass windows in Britain the real standout however is John K Clark's Millennium window after you've toured the cathedral nearby you'll find the necropolis this incredibly atmosphere Cemetery was inspired by the designs of pear Lachey in Paris and it dates back to 1833. it's a lovely albeit slightly morbid place to go for a stroll and look over the city while imagining what the skyline might have looked like a couple centuries ago George square at the center of glasgow's beautiful and historic Victorian City Center stands the flower-covered George Square which features 12 statues of some of the most famous people with ties to the city including Walter Scott Queen Victoria and Robbie Burns at the East End of the square you'll find the town hall with its 230 foot tall tower the tower is a Marvel to behold and was completed in 1890 there's also the Merchant's house which happens to be the headquarters of Britain's oldest Chamber of Commerce it's hard to believe it was founded in 1605 to the south of the square you'll find a collection of mid-19th century warehouses which make up part of glasgow's trendy Merchant City District the district features many unique cafes shops Galleries and restaurants during the holiday season the merchant district is lit up with a dazzling display of Christmas lights and decorations making it one of our favorite places in Scotland to explore the winter months Riverside Museum and Tall ship this award-winning modern museum includes many of the same exhibits formerly featured at the city's now defunct Transport Museum you'll find displays featuring items like model ships vintage cars horse-drawn carriages locomotives and tramps the majority of which were built in Glasgow another highlight is the Museum's period accurate Recreation of a 1938 Glasgow Street there are also exhibits that cover themes like immigration and famous disasters like the sinking of the Lusitania outside is a docked ship called the Glen Lee guests can explore the Glasgow constructed three masted Baroque vessel which was meticulously restored with the help of the Clyde Maritime trust guided tours are available Glasgow Science Center and Glasgow Tower one of the best family-friendly attractions in the city Glasgow Science Center is housed inside a striking titanium building shaped like a hall of a ship inside you'll find exhibits covering everything from Health to technology and scientific principles it's easy to spend the whole day at this seemingly endless Hands-On facility kids can learn the basics of the scientific method at a variety of lab-like stations where they can conduct fun practical and educational experiments the museum additionally features a planetarium IMAX theater and Science Theater where guest speakers regularly give talks and lectures then you have the Glasgow Tower which can be found at the same site the building is the tallest in Scotland standing at 417 feet high from the top at its observation platform you'll be treated to panoramic views of the city and its surrounding landscape what makes the tower all more impressive is the fact that it's capable of rotating a full 360 degrees it was designed in such a way so that it could withstand strong winds it's the tallest rotating building of its kind in the world Pollock house and Pollock Park just about four miles Southwest of Glasgow City Center sits the Pollock house The house's grounds span an area of 355 acres and it was was once the home of the Maxwell family William Adam and his sons built the Edwardian mansion in 1752. today the majority of the building is open for the public to explore Sir William Maxwell's vast collection of Spanish paintings by Velasquez Goya El Greco and Mario hang on display there are also several notable works by William Blake and several other famous painters both guided or self-guided tours are available there's also an interactive exhibit called escape the past where participants try to solve puzzles to make their way back to the modern day Glasgow green and the people's Palace established in 1662 Glasgow green is the oldest Park in the city it's also just a short walk away from the city center one of the Park's primary attractions is what's known as The People's Palace this Museum was built in 1898 and does a great job of telling the detailed story of the history of Glasgow from 1750 through the 21st century one of the exhibits features A full-scale reproduction of a typical single-end home from the 30s there's also a fabulous display dedicated to remembering the old dance hall from the Glasgow Barrel lands Ballroom another must-see attraction is the Winter Garden this massive Conservatory can be found at the rear of the palace and contains the wide selection of both tropical and subtropical plant life make sure to pop over and see the Dalton Fountain which happens to be the largest terracotta fountain in the world at 46 feet high and 70 feet wide the fountain was built to celebrate Queen Victoria's golden jubilee and it features depictions of figures from all across the Commonwealth now it's time to hear from you have you ever been to Glasgow if so did we miss any of your favorite spots if not do you think you'll ever get there let us know in the comments section below foreign

videos.transcript: Glasgow is Scotland's largest city, famed for its Victorian architecture, culture, and of course, people. Given its rugged exterior, Glasgow is often overlooked by international travelers, which is a shame. While most travelers will visit Scotland's capital city of Edinburgh, you have to give Glasgow a chance. It's a city filled with plenty of character and tons to do. We spent 48 hours in Glasgow, and here is some of the top things to do while visiting this amazing city. If you're a Glaswegian, let us know some of your top recommendations. Now, let's jump into the video and see what Glasgow is all about. One of the best ways to view Glasgow is taking a self-guided walking tour. One of the main streets that you're going to come across is Buchanan Street. Buchanan Street is closed to cars, and it's known for its high-end street shopping and beautiful Victorian era architecture. Fun fact, Glasgow is the second largest retail center in the United Kingdom. I highly recommend starting your day here to get a feel for this city. And since you're surrounded by so many locals, keep an ear open to hear the locals talking. The Glaswegians have a unique dialect only found in this city. There's also tons of live music as you walk through Buchanan Street, and Glasgow is one of the best places in Scotland to listen to live music. And since you started your day here, you can check out some other great attractions nearby, like Glasgow Gallery of Modern Art. Before you walk into the museum, you have to check out the Duke of Wellington statue. This traffic cone statue has even inspired Banksy. For decades, Glaswegians have capped the Duke of Wellington with traffic cones. You got to love the Glaswegian sense of humor, and don't ask me why they do it, they just do. We love visiting museums on our travels, and since it started to rain, we decided to check this one out. It's not every day you get to visit a building as old as the United States Declaration of Independence. The GoMA is free to enter and is Scotland's most visited art gallery. After checking out Glasgow Museum of Modern Art, wanted to visit another point of interest in the city and came across multiple street murals. If you're a fan of graffiti, then you'll want to check this one out. I didn't know at the time, but Glasgow was named the fourth best city in the UK for street art. Glasgow has embraced local street artists and giant murals they create, creating splashes of color which brighten up the city streets. So, what do you think about these giant murals in Glasgow? Are you a fan of street art? And if you wanted to check out these diverse and unique pieces of art for yourself, then check out the link in the description for the Glasgow City Centre Mural Trail. Traveling to Glasgow, one of my must things to do is visiting the Glasgow Cathedral. One of the things I love most when traveling is learning about the history of places we visit, and Glasgow is rich with history. When you think of Scotland's history, what comes to mind? Maybe the castles by the loch, battlefields in the Highlands, or beautiful old town of Edinburgh with its towering castles. The Glasgow Cathedral is the only medieval cathedral on the Scottish mainland to survive the Protestant Reformation of 1560 and is firstly intact. As you explore, make sure to read up on the history of the cathedral and admire the Gothic arches and the stained glass windows. It's one of the finest post-war collections of stained glass windows in the United Kingdom. After reading up on some of the history of Glasgow Cathedral, we traveled to the crypt, which was a first for us. The lower levels of the church leads to the tomb of Saint Mungo and tells more about the history of Glasgow Cathedral. Saint Mungo was a 7th-century Scottish monk and mystical founder of Glasgow who established the first wooden church on this very spot. Throughout the crypt, you can see decorative carved pieces of demons, dragons, and skulls. If you're looking for something unique to do in Glasgow, then check this out. And if you're a fan of the show Outlander, then you will recognize this area from the second season. And since Glasgow has a very gothic feel, Glasgow even helped create a very special Gotham City from the Robert Pattinson film The Batman, where he's riding a motorcycle through Glasgow Necropolis. I know what you're thinking. Why would I visit a cemetery? Besides the great views of Glasgow skyline, which is remarkable in itself, the Necropolis is a Victorian garden cemetery full of wonderful architecture, sculptures, and fascinating stories. Over 50,000 burials have taken place here with over 3,500 tombs. If you follow us on our travels, you know we love hiking, and now I can say I hiked through a Necropolis. Walking around Glasgow's City of the Dead is an experience, and the design was inspired by Père Lachaise in Paris, and it is older back to 1833. If you're looking for something unique to do in Glasgow, this is it. Walking around Glasgow can definitely work up your appetite, so we decided to stop by and eat some fish and chips. After eating, we walked over to George Square. George Square is located at the city center and named after King George III, and it was built in 1781. Today, Glasgow Square is home to the headquarters of Glasgow City Council and showcases an important collection of statues and monuments, like the James Watt Memorial, who fathered Europe's Industrial Revolution by improving the steam engine. But what really caught my eye was the George Square Memorial. It's a memorial for Glasgow's fallen during the Great War of 1914 to 1918. It's believed around 200,000 Glaswegians joined up to fight in World War I. We didn't know this at the time, but you can actually tour the city chambers. On our next visit to Glasgow, we're definitely checking that out. And if history's not your thing, then just being in the middle of all the hustle and bustle of Glasgow is worth a visit. The square is in the heart of the city and you can get a better understanding of Glasgow by visiting George Square. What's great about Glasgow that is a walkable city. And since it was raining on and off, we decided to check out the public transportation. We reconnected with my brother Mike, got us a couple coffee, and got on the subway nicknamed Clockwork Orange. It's a simple circular system and easy to navigate. Purchase a day pass for £4 since it's an excellent value. What impressed me was how clean it was. If you visit Glasgow, take the subway and save your money. Did you know that the Glasgow subway system is one of the oldest subway systems in the world? It's even older than the New York City Subway. From a tourist perspective, the Glasgow subway is an ideal way to see the city and we wish we knew about this on day one. We did a self-guided walking tour with my brother Mike, checking out Buchanan Street and walking past Glasgow's Royal Concert Hall. We checked out some bookstores and coffee shops along the way. We even passed by the University of Glasgow, which is a beautiful campus with stunning architecture. If you have the time, do a self-guided tour. Glasgow University is the fourth oldest in the UK and second oldest in Scotland. There's no cost in visiting the campus and it's one of the best sites to view when visiting Glasgow. Our next stop is the Glasgow Botanical Garden, which has been welcoming visitors since 1820. Glasgow Botanical Garden is a great way to spend your day and the cost to enter is free. You can spend a few hours exploring the plants from all over the world. You can tell that Glasgow takes great pride in the Botanical Gardens. The grounds are well cared for and the entire park is a pleasure to walk through. If you're going to do a day trip or spending a few days in Glasgow, I highly recommend checking out the Glasgow Botanic Gardens. From street art to museums, 48 hours of Glasgow's best attractions. Keep in mind that this is not everything to do in Glasgow. There's tons of other things we wanted to do, but we'll save that for our next visit. And if you're a Glaswegian, let us know in the comments some things that we missed. And if you enjoyed the video, give it a like and subscribe to Mr. S Travel Quest. And now to our next destination, the capital city of Edinburgh. Don't forget to subscribe for more travel videos and connect with us on our social media platforms. Thanks for watching and we'll see you in the next video.

videos.description:
Our reporter Hannah travelled to her home country of Scotland to give you her bucketlist of everything you need to check out in the city of Glasgow!
When you think of Glasgow, the first thing that comes to mind is probably cold and rainy weather. But despite that, Scotland's largest city is a vibrant, cultural hub. It's a UNESCO City of Music, and has one of the best live music scenes in the UK.
Which of Hannah's travel tips is your favourite – the museum, the deep-fried Mars bar or the music scene?

videos.transcript: Where can you hang out with musicians, discover beautiful architecture and taste the sweet, but somewhat fishy, local cuisine? It has to be Glasgow! This is my travel bucket list for a day in Scotland's largest city. But don't forget your umbrella! Glasgow is famous for its architecture, both Victorian and modern. And it has a legendary music scene. It's also considered to be one of the world's friendliest cities! Let's go check it out! Tip 1 for the main things to see: architecture. Most of the city as seen today dates back to the 19th century, which is why Glasgow is renowned for its historical architecture. Some of the most famous examples are the university, as well as the Glasgow city chambers. As is... Kelvingrove art museum, one of the most iconic buildings in Glasgow. And the great thing about Scottish museums is that most of them are free! The museum and gallery displays an eclectic mix of art and history, architecture and design. And an impressive concert pipe organ. One of its prized exhibits features the art Nouveau style of Scottish architect Charles Rennie Mackintosh. He is to Glasgow, and Scotland, what Antoni Gaudi is to Barcelona and Spain. His so-called Glasgow style includes the famous ladies tearooms of the late 19th and early 20th century, which were created as a public space for women. Now it's time to try the local cuisine so I'm headed to a fish and chip shop, of course. Glaswegians love their fried food, and today I want to try something I've never had before, despite growing up in Scotland! I'd really like to try this deep-fried mars bar that I keep hearing things about! Yeah sure. It's my first time trying the mars bar. How is it made? If you like, you can come back here and see the process. I discover that the popular Scottish snack is literally just the chocolate bar, dipped in batter and fried in oil. It's then served with ice cream and chocolate sauce – as if it wasn't already sweet enough! Here we have the deep-fried mars bar that I keep hearing really good things about... Actually it tastes really good. Admittedly, it does have a slightly fishy aftertaste though... Now it's time for my must do: discover Glasgow's music scene. The Ben Nevis pub is one of many in the city that hosts regular live music events. Tonight, this group is getting together to play some traditional Scottish folk music. Luc McNally is a professional musician from Newcastle, but has made a home for himself here in Glasgow. It's a vibe. When you're walking down the street, the main stretch of Glasgow streets like Sauchiehallstreet for example, you can walk into loads of bars around midnight and there'll always be something happening with live music. It'll be really great or it'll be terrible or not be too your liking but there's always something happening and it's all dead different so that's great. Well the people here really are as friendly as they claim to be and I've had so many great experiences here in Glasgow. 10 out of 10, would recommend.

videos.transcript: [Music] located on the river clyde glasgow has been transformed from an industrial city to scotland's cultural center with its excellent museums art galleries concert venues and festivals its gaelic name meaning lovely green place is apt given its 70 parks and open spaces number one glasgow cathedral the city's most significant historic building is the 12th century glasgow cathedral also known as mungo cathedral or the high kirk of glasgow seen from both inside and out it looks as if it dropped out of a giant mold the lines are clear and there's no superfluous ornamentation number two glasgow school of art macintosh's art academy is essential viewing for lovers of fine architecture completed in 1909 this art nouveau building confirmed the reputation of 28 year old designer charles mcintosh not just as a master of the exterior the grand west facade is dominated by 365-foot high oreo windows and the smaller windows on the east front are reminiscent of scottish castles but also as a superb interior designer number three kelvin grove art gallery and museum the bustling entertainment and shopping mecca of sochi hall street now almost entirely given over to pedestrians is more than 1.5 miles long and offers the largest range of shops in the city sochi hall street ends at argyle street in the city's west end a trendy area of cafes restaurants high-end shops posh hotels and perhaps most importantly the wonderful calvin grove art gallery and museum number four george square and the merchant district at the heart of glasgow's historic victorian city center stands the flower bedec george square with its 12 statues of famous people associated with the city including robbie burns walter scott and queen victoria number five necropolis neighboring glasgow cathedral is the necropolis a victorian gothic garden cemetery that covers 37 acres that has famously been described as a city of the dead it is filled with not only beautiful memorial stones all told some 3500 of them but also sculptures and buildings designed by glasgow artists including charles rennie macintosh number six riverside museum and tall ship glasgow's ultra-modern award-winning riverside museum includes many of the exhibits from the city's former transport museum including model ships locomotives trams vintage cars and horse-drawn carriages the majority are glasgow built number 7 glasgow science center a must visit sightseeing opportunity for families the glasgow science center is a great way to spend time together located in the waterfront area near the riverside museum this popular attraction is housed in a suitably striking titanium clad building shaped like a ship's hull and offers no end of hands-on fun and exploration number eight pollock house and pollock country park close to four miles southwest of glasgow's city center the grounds of pollock house cover an area of 355 acres the home of the maxwell family this edwardian mansion was built in 1752 by william adam and his sons number nine kibble palace and glasgow botanic gardens built in 1873 kibble palace is one of the largest glass houses in britain and contains a collection of rare orchids tree ferns from australia and new zealand and plants from africa the americas and the far east it is located at glasgow's botanic gardens where you can explore more greenhouses and admire the victorian sculptures located throughout the grounds number 10 glasgow green and the people's palace laid out in 1662 glasgow green is by far the oldest of the city's parks and is an easy walk from the city center one of the park's main attractions is the people's palace a museum built in 1898 that tells the story of glasgow from 1750 through the 20th century hope you videos.transcript: Glasgow is a city where history meets modern creativity, bursting with vibrant culture, stunning architecture, and a thriving music scene. Wander through its historic streets, explore world-class museums, and admire the Victorian grandeur. From the buzzing nightlife to the scenic River Clyde, every corner has something to offer. Whether you're here for the art, the food, or the football, Glasgow's energy and charm make it an unforgettable destination. Today, we share the top 10 best things to do in Glasgow so you can plan a trip and experience all the exciting attractions and activities this beautiful city has to offer. Starting with number 10, Glasgow Botanic Gardens. Glasgow Botanic Gardens is a lush oasis in the city, featuring beautifully curated gardens, scenic walking trails, and a stunning Kibble Palace. Visitors can explore diverse plant collections, including exotic tropical species. The gardens offer a peaceful escape for relaxation and photography. Nature lovers will enjoy the seasonal floral displays, while families can have a fun outdoor experience. The River Kelvin Walkway provides a serene stroll through greenery. At number nine is the Kelvingrove Art Gallery and Museum. Kelvingrove Art Gallery and Museum is a must-visit cultural landmark featuring 22 themed galleries filled with over 8,000 objects. Visitors can marvel at world-renowned paintings, including Salvador Dali's Christ of St. John of the Cross. The museum showcases natural history, medieval armor, and ancient artifacts. Families will love interactive exhibits, while history buffs can explore Glasgow's rich heritage. The grand architecture alone is a sight to behold. Free entry makes it accessible for all. Whether enjoying an organ recital or admiring masterpieces, Kelvingrove offers an unforgettable experience for art and history enthusiasts alike. At number eight, Glasgow Science Centre. The Glasgow Science Centre is an exciting hub featuring interactive exhibits, a planetarium, and a cutting-edge IMAX theater. Visitors can experience hands-on science displays, explore space in the planetarium, or watch immersive 3D films. The Science Mall offers fun experiments and educational exhibits for all ages. Families can engage with robotics, physics, and biology, making science fun and accessible. The iconic titanium-clad building is a landmark. Whether you're fascinated by technology or seeking fun, this attraction offers a thrilling experience. At number seven, the Riverside Museum. The Riverside Museum is Glasgow's award-winning transport museum, home to over 3,000 objects showcasing the evolution of transport. Visitors can explore vintage cars, trams, and locomotives while stepping inside recreated old Glasgow streets. The museum's interactive displays and historical collections make learning about transport history exciting. The tall ship Glenlee, docked outside, offers a glimpse into maritime life. With hands-on exhibits and fascinating stories, it's perfect for history lovers and families alike, offering an engaging experience. At number six in our list, George Square. George Square is the heart of Glasgow, surrounded by grand Victorian architecture and famous statues, including Robert Burns and Queen Victoria. Visitors can soak in the historic atmosphere, enjoy seasonal events, or relax by the fountains. The square hosts festivals, concerts, and Christmas markets, making it a lively cultural hub. With nearby shopping streets like Buchanan Street, it's perfect for sightseeing. Whether you're photographing stunning buildings or enjoying an event, George Square is a vibrant must-visit spot. At number five, the Glasgow Necropolis. The Glasgow Necropolis is a stunning Victorian cemetery with ornate tombstones and impressive sculptures, offering breathtaking city views. Visitors can explore the historic graves of famous Scots while admiring Gothic architecture. The tranquil pathways provide a peaceful walk, and the elevated position offers panoramic views of Glasgow. History lovers will appreciate the stories behind the monuments, while photographers will find striking angles. Whether you're interested in heritage, architecture, or a quiet retreat, the Necropolis offers a fascinating journey through Glasgow's past. At number four, the SSE Hydro. The SSE Hydro is Scotland's top entertainment venue, hosting world-class concerts, sports events, and performances. Visitors can enjoy electrifying live shows featuring global music icons, comedians, and major tournaments. The arena's futuristic design, illuminated at night, is an architectural marvel. With a 14,000 person capacity, the atmosphere is unbeatable. Whether attending a sold-out concert or sports event, the Hydro offers unforgettable experiences. Located by the River Clyde, it's surrounded by restaurants and nightlife, making it a prime entertainment spot. At number three in our list, the University of Glasgow. The University of Glasgow is a historic institution with stunning Gothic architecture. Visitors can walk through its iconic cloisters, explore the Hunterian Museum, or enjoy breathtaking city views. Literature lovers will appreciate its connections to famous alumni like Adam Smith and Lord Kelvin. The University's Harry Potter-like buildings are a favorite for photography and film. Whether exploring its academic history or enjoying the picturesque surroundings, it offers a perfect blend of culture, heritage, and beauty. At number two, the People's Palace and Winter Gardens. The People's Palace and Winter Gardens offer a deep dive into Glasgow's social history, set in the beautiful Glasgow Green. Visitors can explore exhibits showcasing the city's past, from the Industrial Revolution to modern times. The Winter Gardens, filled with exotic plants, provide a peaceful retreat. Families enjoy interactive displays, and history buffs learn about working-class life in Glasgow. The surrounding park is perfect for a relaxing walk, blending history and nature beautifully. Check out the number one places in our list, Glasgow Cathedral and St. Mungo's Museum. Glasgow Cathedral is a breathtaking medieval masterpiece, Scotland's oldest cathedral. Visitors can admire its Gothic architecture, stained-glass windows, and the tomb of St. Mungo. Next door, St. Mungo's Museum explores the city's religious history with exhibits on faith and art. The peaceful surroundings provide a reflective experience. Whether fascinated by history, art, or architecture, this site offers a glimpse into Glasgow's spiritual and cultural heritage, capturing the essence of its past. Now, it's time to hear from you. What's your favorite thing to do in Glasgow? Is there something we missed? Let us know in the comments section below, and don't forget to give this video a like and subscribe to Greenable if you haven't already. Click the bell icon to stay updated on all our latest content.
